coredata.dc:title: Shape-controlled synthesis of CuCo2S4 as highly-efficient electrocatalyst for nonenzymatic detection of H2O2
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Ziyin
Zhang, Sai
Fu, Yanyi
Zheng, Xiaohui
Zheng, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: Controllable synthesis of CuCo2S4 was reported for electrochemical sensing of H2O2. It is the first time that CuCo2S4 was explored as electrocatalyst for H2O2 oxidation. A facile hydrothermal approach is developed for the shape-controlled synthesis of CuCo2S4 by just changing the type of metal precursor. The structures, compositions and electrochemical properties of CuCo2S4 were studied. Electrochemical studies showed that flower-like CuCo2S4 with large surface area and three-dimensional porous structure exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation and an excellent analytical performances with a high sensitivity of 857.1μAmM−1 cm−2, a low detection limit of 0.084μM (S/N=3) and a wide linear range of 4 orders of magnitude were achieved. These results demonstrated that CuCo2S4 was a promising efficient sensing material for electrochemical sensing of H2O2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuCo2S4
Shape-controlled synthesis
H2O2
Nonenzymatic sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) FE-SEM images, (C) EDX spectrum and (D) XRD pattern of flower-like CuCo2S4.
XPS spectrum for (A) Co 2p and (B) Cu 2p.

FE-SEM images of CuCo2S4 obtained by using different metal precursor: (A) Cu(CH3COO)2 and Co(CH3COO)2, (B) CuSO4 and CoSO4, (C) Cu(NO3)2 and Co(NO3)2, (D) CuCl2 and CoCl2, (E) nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of flower-like, particle-like, microsphere-like and nanosheet-like CuCo2S4.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of GCE (a, a’) and f-CuCo2S4/GCE (b, b’) in the absence (a, b) and presence (a’, b’) of 5.0mM H2O2 in 0.2M NaOH at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1, (B) Cyclic voltammograms of f-CuCo2S4/GCE, m-CuCo2S4/GCE, n-CuCo2S4/GCE and p-CuCo2S4/GCE in the presence of 5.0mM H2O2 in 0.2M NaOH at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1, (C) Cyclic voltammograms of f-CuCo2S4/GCE in the presence of 5.0mM H2O2 in different concentrations of NaOH solution at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1, (D) The effect of NaOH concentration on the oxidation peak current of H2O2.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of f-CuCo2S4/GCE in the presence of 5.0mM H2O2 in 0.2M NaOH at different scan rates, (B) Relationship between oxidation peak current and square root of scan rate, (C) Cyclic voltammograms of f-CuCo2S4/GCE in 0.2M NaOH in the presence of H2O2 with different concentrations at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1, (D) Relationship between oxidation peak current and H2O2 concentrations.
(A) Amperometric response of f-CuCo2S4/GCE on successive additions of H2O2 into 0.2M NaOH at 0.5V under constant stirring. (D) Calibration curve of H2O2 versus its concentration.
Amperometric response of f-CuCo2S4/GCE on successive additions of 0.5mM H2O2, 5.0mM KCl, NaNO3, 0.05mM ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and glucose into 0.2M NaOH at 0.5V under constant stirring.
Determination of H2O2 in toothpaste and tap water samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Each value is the average of five measurements.

head.ce:title: Shape-controlled synthesis of CuCo2S4 as highly-efficient electrocatalyst for nonenzymatic detection of H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Analytical Science, Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Controllable synthesis of CuCo2S4 was reported for electrochemical sensing of H2O2. It is the first time that CuCo2S4 was explored as electrocatalyst for H2O2 oxidation. A facile hydrothermal approach is developed for the shape-controlled synthesis of CuCo2S4 by just changing the type of metal precursor. The structures, compositions and electrochemical properties of CuCo2S4 were studied. Electrochemical studies showed that flower-like CuCo2S4 with large surface area and three-dimensional porous structure exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation and an excellent analytical performances with a high sensitivity of 857.1μAmM−1 cm−2, a low detection limit of 0.084μM (S/N=3) and a wide linear range of 4 orders of magnitude were achieved. These results demonstrated that CuCo2S4 was a promising efficient sensing material for electrochemical sensing of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:para: Numerous methods were developed for H2O2 detection since H2O2 played important roles in food industry, bioanalysis and many other fields [1,2]. Among various methods [3–5], electrochemical method received much attention. Electrochemical sensor usually worked based on the reduction or oxidation of H2O2 on electrode surface since the reduction or oxidation of H2O2 would lead to current response and the current response had a relationship with H2O2 concentration. However, the direct reduction or oxidation of H2O2 on bare electrode surface usually required high overpotential and only resulted in less current response. The large overpotential and low current response made it difficult to obtain an excellent selectivity and sensitivity [6]. Therefore, modifying bare electrode with suitable sensing material to reduce overpotential and enhance current response was desired.

Various modified electrodes were reported, in which modifying bare electrode with enzymes to fabricate enzyme-based biosensors could ensure good selectivity. However, the instability and complicated immobilization procedure limited their applications [7]. Comparing with enzyme-based biosensor, nonenzymatic sensors were usually fabricated with nanomaterials and the presence of nanomaterials not only exhibited catalytic activity toward H2O2 reduction or oxidation but also made nonenzymatic sensors possess advantages of high stability and simple immobilization procedure [8]. Until now, numerous novel metals [9,10], metal oxide [11,12], metal sulfide [13] and other materials [14] have been employed as electrocatalysts for fabricating nonenzymatic H2O2 sensors. However, despite these studies, searching for novel sensing material which not only is low cost but also possesses excellent electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation is still desired.

As a mixed-metal sulfide, CuCo2S4 exhibited interesting electronic, catalytic and magnetic properties and these properties made it wide applications in the fields of supercapacitors, oxygen evolution reaction and rechargeable lithium-ion batteries [15]. Recently, various Cu and Co compound materials have been employed as sensing materials for electrochemical sensing [11,12]. However, to the best of our knowledge, CuCo2S4 has not been studied as sensing material and the application of CuCo2S4 as electrocatalyst for electrochemical sensing of H2O2 has not been explored. Moreover, the morphology of material has an influence on its properties [16]. Therefore, it may be possible to tailor the electrochemical properties of CuCo2S4 by controlling its morphologies. A few studies had reported the synthesis of CuCo2S4 with well-defined morphologies. However, in the synthesis of CuCo2S4, template [17] or structure-directing agent [18] were usually required, which made the preparation method complicated and limited the wide applications of CuCo2S4 in some extent. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a facile approach for controllable synthesis of CuCo2S4 and then explore its electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation.

In this paper, CuCo2S4 with different morphologies were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach through changing the type of metal precursor and then for the first time CuCo2S4 was used as excellent catalyst for electrochemical sensing of H2O2. The results showed that CuCo2S4 possessed excellent catalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation with a wide linear range, a high sensitivity and a low detection limit.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), thiourea and ethylene glycol were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Research Institute. Cobalt chloride (CoCl2·6H2O), cobalt acetate (Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O), cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), cobalt sulfate (CoSO4·7H2O), copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O), copper acetate (Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O), copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) and copper sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pure double distilled water was used in all experiments.

Field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images and X ray diffraction (XRD) pattern were obtained by Carl Zeiss microscope (SIGMA VP, Germany) and D/MAX 3C (Rigaku, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was measured on an ESCALAB MK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg as the exciting source. CHI 660 electrochemical workstation (China) was employed to study the electrochemical properties, in which glassy carbon electrode, saturated calomel electrode and platinum wire were used as working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively.

CuCo2S4 was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. CuCl2·2H2O (0.0426g), CoCl2·6H2O (0.119g) and thiourea (0.114g) were added into 30.0mL of ethylene glycol under vigorous magnetic stirring. After 20min, the above solution was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 200°C for 10h. When Teflon-lined autoclave was cooled, the product was obtained by centrifugation and washing out with distilled water.

The modified electrode was fabricated by casting method. 1.0mL of acetic acid (37% (m:m)) was added into 99.0mL of water and then 0.5g chitosan was added into above solution. Then, CuCo2S4 (2.0mg) was added into chitosan solution (1.0mL, 0.5% (m:m)) to obtain a homogeneous dispersion. Chitosan was used as the binding reagent for CuCo2S4 immobilization because of its excellent film-forming ability, good adhesion and high mechanical strength. Then, CuCo2S4 solution (6.0μL) was dropped on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) which had been polished by alumina powder. The modified electrode was expressed as CuCo2S4/GCE and its electrochemical properties were studied after dried in air at room temperature.

FE-SEM images were recorded to study the morphologies of CuCo2S4. Fig. 1 (A, B) display FE-SEM images of CuCo2S4 which was synthesized by using CuCl2 and CoCl2 as metal precursors at 200°Cfor 10h. As shown in Fig. 1 (A, B), large numbers of flower-like structures with the diameter of about 3μm were observed and these flower-like structures looked like to be assembled by nanosheets. Such three-dimensional structure not only preserved large active surfaces of nanosheets but also provided abundant spaces and channels for the transport of electrolytes and target molecules, therefore allowing them to efficiently contact with more active surfaces. Moreover, in order to investigate the compositions and structure of CuCo2S4, EDX spectrum and XRD pattern were shown in Fig. 1 (C, D). The EDX spectrum of CuCo2S4 (Fig. 1 (C)) confirmed the presence of Cu, Co and S element and the atomic ratio of Cu, Co and S was approximately 1:2:4 and this was in good agreement with the stoichiometric ratio of CuCo2S4. The XRD pattern in Fig. 1 (D) displayed several peaks at 26.55°, 31.36°, 37.94°, 47.11°, 50.09° and 54.91° which were in agreement with (022), (113), (004), (224), (115) and (044) planes of CuCo2S4 [19]. In order to give a more comprehensive estimation on the states of metals in CuCo2S4, XPS experiments were recorded in Fig. 2 (A, B). As shown in Fig. 2 (A), two prominent peaks at binding energies of 778.3 and 793.4eV appeared and they could correspond to the Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks [20], respectively. This confirmed the existence of Co2+ and Co3+. The Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2 (B)) presented two peaks at 932.2 and 952.1eV, which could be assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 and indicated the presence of Cu+ [17]. CuCo2S4 possessed mixed valence of Co and Cu, which was beneficial to provide abundant active sites for reaction. Therefore, the results of FE-SEM images, EDX spectrum, XRD pattern and XPS experiments demonstrated the successful synthesis of flower-like CuCo2S4.

CuCo2S4 with well-defined morphologies have been synthesized by choosing appropriate of surfactant/stabilizer, solvents or template. However, metal precursor may also affect the morphologies of CuCo2S4 and the influence of metal precursor has not explored. Fig. 3 show the morphologies of CuCo2S4 which were synthesized by using different metal precursors. From Fig. 3 (A), it can be seen that large numbers of particles with diameter of 200nm were obtained when using Cu(CH3COO)2 and Co(CH3COO)2 as metal precursor. However, with CuSO4 and CoSO4 as copper and cobalt source (Fig. 3 (B)), some irregular CuCo2S4 microspheres were observed and such microspheres were assembled by nanoparticles. Interestingly, the product obtained with Cu(NO3)2 and Co(NO3)2 as metal precursors displayed irregular sheet-like structures with some distorted flower-like structures (Fig. 3 (C)). As shown in Fig. 3 (D), only using CuCl2 and CoCl2 as metal precursors, CuCo2S4 with well flower-like structures could be obtained. This indicated that metal precursor had an influence on the morphologies of CuCo2S4 and the morphologies of CuCo2S4 could be controlled by using different metal precursors [21]. Moreover, the specific surface areas of CuCo2S4 with different morphologies were studied by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. Fig. 3 (E) shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of flower-like, particle-like, microsphere-like and nanosheet-like CuCo2S4 and the specific surface areas of flower-like, particle-like, microsphere-like and nanosheet-like CuCo2S4 were calculated to be 18.76m2g−1, 7.29m2g−1, 6.35m2g−1 15.72m2g−1, respectively. Therefore, among various CuCo2S4 structures, flower-like CuCo2S4 possessed larger surface areas and the larger surface areas were beneficial to provide more active sites for H2O2 reaction.

The catalytic activity of CuCo2S4 toward H2O2 oxidation was studied. GCE were modified with flower-like, particle-like, microsphere-like and nanosheet-like CuCo2S4, which were expressed as f-CuCo2S4/GCE, p-CuCo2S4/GCE, m-CuCo2S4/GCE and n-CuCo2S4/GCE, respectively. Fig. 4 (A) shows cyclic voltammetry (CV) of GCE (a, a’) and f-CuCo2S4/GCE (b, b’) in the absence (a, b) and presence (a’, b’) of 5.0mM H2O2 in 0.2M NaOH at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1. As shown in Fig. 4 (A), in the case of GCE, no obvious oxidation or reduction peak was observed in the absence of H2O2 and only low oxidation current appeared after adding H2O2. This indicated that there was no electroactive substance on the surface of GCE and GCE possessed less electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation. However, in the absence of H2O2, f-CuCo2S4/GCE (b) displayed several broad redox peaks from 0.3V to 0.6V which may be ascribed to the transitions of Co(II)/Co(III), Co(III)/Co(IV) and Cu(II)/Cu(III) [22]. This indicated that flower-like CuCo2S4 was an electroactive substance and also confirmed that flower-like CuCo2S4 was successfully modified on GCE. Interestingly, after adding H2O2, the anodic current notablely increased with the decreasing of cathodic current, indicating that f-CuCo2S4/GCE exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation. Moreover, comparing with that of other CuCo2S4 structures (Fig. 4 (B)), the highest oxidation peak current was obtained at f-CuCo2S4/GCE. This implied that flower-like CuCo2S4 possessed superior electrocatalytic activity and also demonstrated that the morphology of CuCo2S4 could influence on its electrochemical properties in some extent. The excellent electrocatalytic activity of flower-like CuCo2S4 may be due to that CuCo2S4 belonged to binary metal sulfides with two metal cations and could offer abundant redox ability and active sites for reaction. In addition, flower-like CuCo2S4 exhibited three-dimensional porous structure. Such structure not only preserved large active surfaces of nanosheets but also provided abundant spaces and channels for the transport of OH− and H2O2 molecules, therefore allowing them to efficiently contact with active surfaces. The possible electrooxidation mechanism of H2O2 may be expressed as follows:
CuCo2O4 +OH− +H2O→CuOOH+2CoOOH+e−
CoOOH+OH− →CoO2 +H2O+e−
2CoOOH+H2O2 →2Co(OH)2 +O2
2CoO2 +H2O2 →2CoOOH+O2
2CuOOH+H2O2 →2Cu(OH)2 +O2

Moreover, the influence of NaOH concentration on the electrocatalytic activity of f-CuCo2S4/GCE toward H2O2 oxidation was studied. Fig. 4 (C) shows CVs of f-CuCo2S4/GCE in the presence of 5.0mM H2O2 in different concentrations of NaOH solution at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1. Fig. 4 (D) reveals the effect of NaOH concentration on the oxidation peak current of H2O2. From Fig. 4 (C and D), it can be seen that the oxidation peak current increased with the increase of NaOH concentration first and then the oxidation peak current increased little when the concentration was further increased. Considering that some intermediates usually generated in excessive concentration of NaOH solution and high concentration of NaOH solution was also not eco-friendly, therefore 0.2M NaOH was chosen as the support electrolyte for the electrochemical detection of H2O2.

The effect of scan rate on the electrocatalytic activity of f-CuCo2S4/GCE was further investigated by recording CVs in the presence of H2O2 under different scan rate. As shown in Fig. 5 (A), with successive increasing of scan rate, the anodic peak current increased. Moreover, a liner relationship between oxidation peak current and square root of scan rate was obtained (Fig. 5 (B)), which indicated that the oxidation of H2O2 at the surface of f-CuCo2S4/GCE may be controlled by diffusion process. Fig. 5 (C) reveals CVs of f-CuCo2S4/GCE in different concentrations of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 5 (C), the oxidation current gradually enhanced with the increasing of H2O2 concentration from 0mM to 5mM, demonstrating excellent electrocatalytic activity of f-CuCo2S4/GCE toward different H2O2 concentrations. Moreover, Fig. 5 (D) displays a good linear relationship between oxidation peak current and H2O2 concentration, which meant that it may be possible to employ f-CuCo2S4/GCE for H2O2 detection in a wide liner range.

Amperometric I-t experiment was performed to study the sensing property of f-CuCo2S4/GCE. Fig. 6 (A) shows the amperometric response of f-CuCo2S4/GCE on successive addition of H2O2 at the working potential of 0.5V. As shown in Fig. 6 (A), the addition of H2O2 could lead to quick current response. With successive addition of H2O2, the current response increased accordingly. Moreover, from Fig. 6 (B), it was found that f-CuCo2S4/GCE exhibited three different linear relationships in the ranges 0.5μM − 0.0275mM, 0.0275mM − 0.2775mM, 0.2775mM − 2.278mM with slopes of 60.0, 31.92 and 15.70, respectively. Therefore, three sensitivities of 857.1μAmM−1 cm−2, 456.0μAmM−1 cm−2 and 224.3μAmM−1 cm−2 were obtained according to the slope and geometric surface area of GCE. After adding high concentrations of H2O2, the sensitivity gradually decreased. This may be due to that the surface of f-CuCo2S4/GCE was partially covered by reaction intermediates and made f-CuCo2S4/GCE difficult to provide enough sites for high concentrations of H2O2. In addition, the low detection limit was estimated to be 0.084μM based on the equation LOD=3SB/b, in which SB represented the standard deviation of the blank solution and b was the slope of calibration curve. In order to compare with other nonenzymatic H2O2 sensors, the sensing properties of f-CuCo2S4/GCE were added in Table 1 . Table 1 reveals that the sensing properties of f-CuCo2S4/GCE were good in terms of liner range, sensitivity and detection limit. The good sensing properties may be benefited from abundant redox ability, active sites and three-dimensional porous structure of flower-like CuCo2S4.

Apart from the sensing properties of liner range, sensitivity and detection limit, the selectivity of f-CuCo2S4/GCE was investigated by recording the amperometric response of f-CuCo2S4/GCE toward H2O2 and some common interfering species (Fig. 7 ). As shown in Fig. 7, comparing with the large current response resulted from the addition of H2O2, the current response was low after adding interfering reagents, indicating the good anti-interference behavior of f-CuCo2S4/GCE. Moreover, as important parameters of one sensor, the reproducibility and stability of f-CuCo2S4/GCE were explored. Six f-CuCo2S4/GCE were fabricated for detecting H2O2 and the relative standard deviation was calculated to be 7.3%. Moreover, f-CuCo2S4/GCE was stored in air at room temperature when not in use. The amperometric response of f-CuCo2S4/GCE toward H2O2 was tested over 6days and the current response obtained at sixth day was found to be 80.3% of its initial current response. Therefore, the combinations of excellent electrocatalytic activity, good selectivity, acceptable reproducibility and stability confirmed that flower-like CuCo2S4 was an efficient electrocatalyst for H2O2 oxidation and also indicated that f-CuCo2S4/GCE may be possible to be used for practical analysis.

The practical application of f-CuCo2S4/GCE was explored. 0.001g toothpaste was diluted with 1.0mL 0.2M NaOH and then certain concentrations of H2O2 were added into above solution. The results of recovery measurements showed that recoveries were between 90.7% and 96.4% (Table 2 ). Moreover, standard concentrations of H2O2 were added into tap water samples and the recovery measurements showed that the recoveries obtained were acceptable. These indicated that the substances in toothpaste or tap water had little influence on the detection of H2O2 and f-CuCo2S4/GCE had the potential for applications in practical analysis.

CuCo2S4 had been explored and proven as a highly-efficient electrocatalyst for H2O2 oxidation, where CuCo2S4 with different morphologies were successfully synthesized via a facile hydrothermal approach without requiring template, structure-directing agent or complicated steps. Among various CuCo2S4 structures, flower-like CuCo2S4 with abundant redox ability and three-dimensional porous structure provided more active sites for H2O2 reaction and also offered many channels for the transport of H2O2, therefore making flower-like CuCo2S4 modified electrode exhibit an excellent performance for H2O2 detection. This study developed a facile approach for the shape-controlled synthesis of CuCo2S4 and provided an attractive electrocatalyst for H2O2 oxidation, which may paved the way for the facial synthesis of other binary metal sulfides with unique morphology and also opened new avenues for the potential application of CuCo2S4 in the aspect of electrochemical sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this project by the National Science Fundation of China (No. 21575113), the Natural Science Fundation of Shaanxi Province in China (No. 2013KJXX-25), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory (No. 15JS100, 16JS099).
